original_abstract:
This article provides a practical-theological reflection on the importance of coaching and equipping children for service as a primary means of emulating the attitude of Christ. At the heart of this research is the hypothesis that children are not merely the future of the church, but an inherent and active part of the body of Christ today. Like all believers, children receive spiritual gifts intended for the edification of the church; consequently, the faith community bears a significant responsibility to mentor and accompany them as they navigate their calling to follow Jesus.

Through the lens of *diakonia*, this study argues that service is a vocational response to God's calling. To explore this, the research follows a four-phase methodology. First, the authors offer a descriptive reflection on current trends within practical theology. Second, an intra-disciplinary dialogue with developmental and social psychology is utilized to identify how negative attitudes and insufficient guidance currently hinder a child’s spiritual growth. Third, the study establishes a normative "basic-theoretical" framework rooted in Scripture to outline the ideal praxis of childhood ministry. Finally, the authors propose practical-theoretical perspectives to assist churches in equipping children for their own unique ministries.

The research concludes that when children are empowered to serve in obedience to Christ, they become capable of reflecting His character to the world. By actively mentoring children in their *diakonia*, parents and adult believers do more than provide instruction—they foster a pathway toward genuine spiritual maturity and lifelong discipleship.
humanized_abstract:
Coaching and equipping children for Service as an attitude of following Christ: A practical-theological investigation This article is an investigation into the practical-theology of coaching and equipping children for service as an attitude of following Christ. The underlying assumption for this investigation is that children are not only the future of the church, but that they are members of the body of Christ in the here and now. In the same way that all members of the body of Christ receive gifts for the up-building of the church, so too do children receive gifts of service from God. Therefore, it is the responsibility of the church to guide and to walk with them as they discover their call to follow Christ.

Service in the Context of Diakonia. The study deals with service as a vocation to which God calls. The study is carried out by means of a four-stage methodological procedure. The initial stage involves a descriptive reflection on the development of practical theology. In the second stage an intra-disciplinary dialogue is carried out with developmental and social psychology in order to understand how negative attitudes and insufficient guidance hinder a child's spiritual development. In the third stage a basic-theoretical normative theory is built on the basis of the Bible in order to determine the ideal practice of work with children. In the fourth stage the author presents practical-theoretical principles in order to guide the church in preparing children for their own unique ministry.

That as children are commissioned for service to Jesus, they can be used as a representation of Jesus in the world. And that in mentoring children to participate in *diakonia* (service), parents and adults model a way not only of spiritual education, but of ongoing spiritual development and discipleship.
